metadata.summary: LangChain observability: LangSmith tracing, custom callbacks, streaming, and AgentExecutor gotchas for debugging production chains.

content.title: LangChain Observability
content.summary: LangChain observability requires LangSmith tracing enabled from the start (not retroactively), custom callbacks for structured logging, and streaming for low-latency UX. Production agents running without tracing are nearly impossible to debug retrospectively — the full execution trace including intermediate steps is only captured during the run.

why.p: LangSmith tracing is opt-in but easy to forget; adding it after deployment misses the history needed for debugging. Once enabled, every chain.invoke() call produces a full trace including input, output, intermediate steps, token counts, and latency. The @traceable decorator wraps non-LangChain functions into the same trace tree, giving end-to-end visibility across hybrid pipelines.

Custom callbacks (BaseCallbackHandler subclasses) are the right hook for structured logging, cost tracking, and alerting. They run synchronously in the chain's execution context and receive typed events (on_llm_start, on_llm_end, on_chain_start, on_tool_start) rather than raw text.

list.item: Any LangChain chain deployed to production where debugging chain failures requires execution traces.
Building conversational agents where session-level cost and latency monitoring is required.
When LangSmith tracing is required for production observability.
Streaming use cases where tokens must be yielded incrementally to the client.
Throwaway scripts or local experiments where tracing overhead is not worth the setup.
Air-gapped environments where LangSmith's SaaS endpoint is unreachable — self-hosted LangSmith is an option but adds operational complexity.

section.name: langsmith-tracing
section.p: LangSmith auto-instruments all LangChain chains when LANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2 is set. The @traceable decorator adds non-LangChain functions to the same trace. Every run is recorded: input, output, tokens, cost, latency, and intermediate steps.

rule.statement: Enable LangSmith tracing from the start of development, not retroactively; adding it after deployment misses the history needed for debugging.
rule.rationale: Tracing captures the full execution tree at invocation time. Retroactive tracing cannot reconstruct past failures. The cost of tracing is negligible compared to the cost of debugging a production failure blind.

example.title: LangSmith tracing with @traceable decorator
example.code:
import os from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser from langsmith import traceable
# Enable tracing at startup, not retroactively os.environ["LANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2"] = "true" os.environ["LANGCHAIN_API_KEY"] = "your-api-key" os.environ["LANGCHAIN_PROJECT"] = "my-project"
llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o", temperature=0) prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([ ("system", "You are a helpful assistant."), ("user", "{input}") ]) chain = prompt | llm | StrOutputParser()
# All chain.invoke() calls are now traced automatically
# Wrap non-LangChain logic into the same trace @traceable def my_function(query: str) -> str: return chain.invoke({"input": query})

antipattern.description: Adding LangSmith tracing only after a production incident occurs to "see what happened".
antipattern.reason: Tracing captures execution at invocation time. Past runs without tracing cannot be reconstructed. Enable it from day one.

section.name: custom-callbacks
section.p: BaseCallbackHandler subclasses receive typed lifecycle events from chains, LLMs, and tools. They run synchronously in the chain's execution context. Use them for structured logging, cost tracking, latency measurement, and alerting — not for modifying chain inputs or outputs.

rule.statement: AgentExecutor with verbose=True prints internal reasoning to stdout; in production this leaks intermediate state — disable verbose=True and use callbacks for structured logging instead.
rule.rationale: Verbose stdout output exposes intermediate reasoning steps to logs that may be accessible to downstream consumers. Callbacks produce structured, filterable events instead of raw stdout.

example.title: Custom callback for structured logging

section.name: streaming
section.p: LCEL chains support streaming natively via chain.stream() (sync) and chain.astream() (async). Each chunk is a string token from StrOutputParser or a partial dict from other parsers. Streaming requires an LLM that supports it; set streaming=True on the model or use a callback.

example.title: Sync and async streaming
example.code:
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser from langchain_core.callbacks import StreamingStdOutCallbackHandler
llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o", temperature=0) prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([ ("system", "You are a helpful assistant."), ("user", "{input}") ]) chain = prompt | llm | StrOutputParser()
# Sync streaming — yields tokens as they arrive for chunk in chain.stream({"input": "Tell me a story"}): print(chunk, end="", flush=True)
# Async streaming — use in async contexts (FastAPI, websockets) async def stream_response(query: str): async for chunk in chain.astream({"input": query}): yield chunk
# Alternative: streaming via callback on the LLM streaming_llm = ChatOpenAI( model="gpt-4o", streaming=True, callbacks=[StreamingStdOutCallbackHandler()] )

section.name: agent-executor-gotchas
section.p: AgentExecutor is the standard wrapper for tool-using agents. Its verbose flag, session ID management, and Pydantic error handling are common sources of production surprises.

rule.statement: Session IDs for RunnableWithMessageHistory must be unique per user session; reusing session IDs across users merges conversation histories.
rule.rationale: RunnableWithMessageHistory stores history keyed by session_id. If two users share a session_id, their messages interleave in each other's history, causing context pollution and potential data leakage.

rule.statement: Pydantic validation errors in PydanticOutputParser are raised as exceptions, not returned as structured errors; catch them explicitly in the chain or use a fallback.
rule.rationale: An uncaught PydanticOutputParser exception propagates up the chain and returns a 500 to the caller rather than a degraded structured response.

example.title: AgentExecutor without verbose in production
example.code:
from langchain.agents import create_openai_tools_agent, AgentExecutor from langchain_core.tools import tool
@tool def get_weather(location: str) -> str: """Get the current weather for a location.""" return f"Weather in {location}: Sunny, 72F"
tools = [get_weather]
# max_iterations caps runaway agents; verbose=False keeps logs clean agent_executor = AgentExecutor( agent=agent, tools=tools, verbose=False, # never True in production max_iterations=5 )
response = agent_executor.invoke({"input": "What's the weather in Paris?"})

antipattern.description: Running AgentExecutor with verbose=True in a production service behind an API.
antipattern.reason: verbose=True prints internal reasoning steps to stdout, leaking intermediate agent state into application logs. Use callbacks for structured, filterable event capture instead.
